content:
Hello, I'm CosmoLab AI — your strategy consultant for today's session. I'm here to build a complete picture of your company's current strategic position and help surface the clearest opportunities for growth. This will take about 30 minutes.

Let's start with the fundamentals. Can you give me a brief overview of the business — approximate annual revenue, what product categories you manufacture, and what type of clients you primarily serve?

We're about $85 million in annual revenue. Our mix is roughly 60% skincare, 25% haircare, and 15% OTC drug products. Our clients are a mix — about 40% are established mid-market brands, 35% are indie brands, and 25% are enterprise or retail private label.
Good — a diversified category and client mix. What's been the revenue trajectory over the last two to three years? And which categories or client segments are driving growth versus declining?

We've grown from $68M to $85M over 3 years — about 7.5% CAGR. Skincare is the engine, especially premium anti-aging and clean beauty. OTC has been flat — we're capacity constrained there. Haircare is growing but at a lower margin than skincare. Indie brands are our fastest growing segment but also highest churn.

7.5% CAGR is solid for a CM in this market. On capacity — you mentioned OTC is constrained. What's your overall capacity utilization across the business? And where do you have headroom to grow without additional capital investment?

Our skincare lines are running at about 85% utilization — we have some headroom but not a lot. OTC is at 95% plus, which is why we're losing business there. Haircare is at about 70%. We have a $12M capex plan to expand OTC capacity and add a new high-speed skincare filling line — we expect that to come online in 18 months.

So OTC expansion is already in motion, which removes one constraint. What categories or formats is the market asking for that you currently can't serve — and how much business have you turned away in the last 12 months?

We've turned away an estimated $8-10M in supplements business because we don't have a softgel or encapsulation capability. We also get requests for aerosol products — dry shampoos, setting sprays — and we have no aerosol line. And we're seeing strong demand for APAC-compliant formulations, especially for K-beauty inspired products, but our regulatory expertise is US and EU focused.

That's a significant opportunity cost — $8–10M in identified turns-away in supplements alone. Let's talk about client concentration. What do your top 3 to 5 clients represent as a percentage of revenue?
Our top client is about 18% of revenue. Top 5 combined is about 45%. We've been working on diversification — 3 years ago the top 5 was 58%, so we've made progress but we're still exposed.
Good progress on concentration. Where do you win competitively — and where do you lose deals? What are the one or two reasons clients choose you over another CM?

We win on formulation capability and speed — our R&D team is strong and we can turn around a prototype in 3 weeks versus the industry average of 6–8. We also win on regulatory depth for US and EU markets. We lose deals on price when clients are purely cost-driven, and we lose on format capability — the aerosol and supplements gaps cost us deals every quarter.

Speed and formulation quality are powerful differentiators — those are hard to replicate. On geographic expansion — you mentioned APAC regulatory gaps. Is international expansion a strategic priority, or is the focus domestic growth for the next 12 months?

It's on the radar but not an immediate priority. We have a few clients asking us to support their APAC launches and we keep having to refer them out. We'd like to build that capability but it's not in this year's plan.
Understood — an emerging priority rather than an active investment. Finally, any M&A, partnership, or technology investments currently under consideration beyond the $12M capex?

We've had informal conversations with a small supplements CM — they have the encapsulation capability we lack. A bolt-on acquisition could solve the supplements gap faster than building. We're also looking at upgrading our ERP and considering whether to invest in a formulation management system to replace our current Coptis setup.

That's a strategically interesting acquisition angle — capability acquisition rather than revenue acquisition. And the Coptis replacement aligns with the digital transformation work.

I have everything I need. Type 'generate' to create your strategic assessment.
